title: Sports Hydration Electrolytes Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Optimal Hydration & Electrolyte Intake for Exercise
description: Calculate your ideal hydration: 500-1000ml/h with 400-800mg sodium for exercise. Free, fast calculator updated 2026.
intro: Reference values for athletic hydration come from research organizations like the American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) and WHO. These give you a framework to understand your needs, but your personal situation comes first. For exercise lasting over 1 hour: drink 500–1000ml per hour with 400–800mg of sodium. These values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you informed.
keyTakeaway: **Sports Hydration Formula**: Calculate your hydration needs based on exercise duration and intensity. For workouts over 1 hour: aim for 500–1000ml per hour with 400–800mg sodium.
useCases: Calculate precise hydration requirements for endurance athletes
Plan electrolyte replacement during training and competition
Verify hydration strategies before long-distance events
Teaching sports nutrition and exercise physiology concepts
Quick reference for coaches and fitness professionals
Prevent dehydration during sustained physical activity
explanation:
**Understanding normal health values** comes from large population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). These vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. They're guideline values; your personal medical history always takes priority.

## How It's Calculated

For exercise lasting more than 1 hour: 500–1000ml per hour with 400–800mg of sodium.

This calculator applies the formula with your input values, checking for typical ranges and alerting you to any values that seem unusual or outside safe parameters.

## Normal Reference Values for Adults

| Parameter | Normal Range |
|---|---|
| Blood Pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting Heart Rate | 60–100 bpm |
| Body Temperature | 36.1–37.2°C |
| Oxygen Saturation (SpO2) | 95–100% |
| Fasting Blood Glucose | 70–100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total Cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL | >40 (M) / >50 (F) |

## Final Notes

This calculation is a guideline reference only. For critical decisions (medical, financial, legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: How much water should I drink during exercise?
faq.a: For activities lasting over 1 hour, aim for 500–1000ml per hour depending on intensity, climate, and individual sweat rate. Start hydrating before you feel thirsty.

faq.q: Why do I need sodium during long workouts?
faq.a: Sodium helps your body retain fluid and maintains electrolyte balance. Aim for 400–800mg per hour during extended exercise. Plain water alone can lead to hyponatremia in endurance events.

faq.q: Do men and women need different hydration amounts?
faq.a: Hydration needs depend more on exercise intensity, duration, and sweat rate than gender. Use the same general guidelines adjusted for your individual needs and sweat response.

faq.q: How do I calculate my personal sweat rate?
faq.a: Weigh yourself before and after 1 hour of exercise (without drinking). The difference in weight equals your sweat loss. Use this to personalize your hydration strategy.

faq.q: Can you drink too much water during exercise?
faq.a: Yes. Drinking excessive water without electrolytes can cause hyponatremia (dangerously low blood sodium). Follow the 500–1000ml/hour guideline and include electrolytes in your fluids.

faq.q: Is this calculator accurate for my specific sport?
faq.a: These are general guidelines based on exercise science research. Your actual needs may vary based on climate, fitness level, genetics, and sport-specific factors. Use this as a starting point and adjust based on experience.

faq.q: What if I'm exercising in hot or cold weather?
faq.a: Heat increases fluid and sodium losses. In hot conditions, you may need more than 1000ml/hour. Cold weather reduces sweat rate, so you may need less. Always adjust for environmental conditions.

faq.q: Is my data stored when I use this calculator?
faq.a: No. All calculations happen in your browser. We don't store, transmit, or track any of your personal data.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
